one_line_takeaway: A safety/robustness (not security) autonomous-driving paper that distills a VLM's structured long-tail hazard and planning cues into a lightweight trajectory planner, reporting the best RFS (6.81) and lowest displacement errors on annotated WOD-E2E, with ablations showing motion history matters most.
key_finding: On the annotated WOD-E2E validation split, HERMES achieves the best Rater Feedback Score (RFS, the benchmark's primary human-aligned metric) of 6.81 and the lowest displacement errors among all baselines: ADE@3s 0.82, ADE@5s 1.82, FDE@3s 1.73, FDE@5s 4.81 - substantially below the best baseline LightEMMA (RFS 6.11; ADE@5s 5.41; FDE@5s 11.32). Category-wise, HERMES leads global RFS (6.81 vs LightEMMA 6.11) and wins most of the ten scenario categories, with notable gains where semantic reasoning matters (single-lane maneuvers 7.28, construction 7.26, pedestrian 7.11), though it does not dominate every category (e.g. FOD 6.37, Others 6.41). Ablations show the historical state is the single most important component (removing it drops RFS to 5.51), followed by the textual long-tail instructions (No Instruction -> 6.21) and intent conditioning (No Intent -> 6.56).
core_contribution: HERMES is a risk-aware end-to-end autonomous-driving framework that injects explicit long-tail semantic cues into trajectory planning via a teacher-student design. A cloud VLM (Qwen3-VL-Flash) annotates the WOD-E2E dataset with two structured, pre-computed offline signals - a Long-Tail Scene Context (multi-view hazard-centric descriptions: occlusions, abnormal behaviors, rare objects, adverse conditions) and a Long-Tail Planning Context (risk level, maneuver intent, high-level directive, rationale). These are encoded by BGE-M3 into text embeddings and fed to a lightweight 'Tri-Modal Driving Module' student that fuses multi-view camera features, historical ego-motion, and the long-tail instruction embeddings to output a 5s trajectory. The authors also contribute the annotation pipeline/prompt template and the annotated dataset itself. Note: the 'safety' and 'risk-aware' framing is about physical driving safety in rare traffic scenarios, NOT adversarial/cyber security; there is no attacker and no threat model in the paper.
threat_model: None. This is not a security paper. Despite the ingestion cluster label 'Autonomous Vehicle Security', HERMES defines no adversary, no attack surface, and no defense against malicious manipulation. 'Risk' throughout means physical collision/safety risk from rare (long-tail) traffic events (pedestrian incursions, cut-ins, occlusions, adverse weather), which are statistically underrepresented in training data - a robustness/generalization problem, not a security one.
limitations: Open-loop evaluation only (MSE-to-trajectory / RFS), no closed-loop or on-vehicle deployment despite the 'embodied system' framing. Long-tail contexts are pre-computed offline and treated as fixed training inputs, sidestepping real-time VLM latency and cloud-dependency at inference. Baselines are unevenly matched: LightEMMA is run zero-shot without fine-tuning, while UniAD and VAD were only 'limitedly adapted' to the WOD-E2E interface, making the head-to-head comparison favorable to HERMES and hard to interpret cleanly. Evaluation is on the validation split (479 segments) because official test-set access is restricted, so no held-out test numbers. Only 3 training epochs on a single GPU. No statistical significance/variance reported, no reproducibility of the VLM annotations quantified beyond 'human verification'. The paper's 'first framework' novelty claims sit alongside a Related Work section citing very similar VLM/MLLM driving systems (GPT-Driver, DriveVLM, EMMA, LMDrive, DiMA), so the delta is the explicit long-tail risk-context injection rather than the VLM-for-driving idea itself.

datasets_used: WOD-E2E (Waymo Open Dataset End-to-End): 4,021 driving segments, ~12 hours real-world urban/suburban driving; official splits 2,037 train / 479 validation / 1,505 test; authors add offline Long-Tail Scene Context and Long-Tail Planning Context annotations on top
Evaluation performed on the WOD-E2E validation split (479 segments) due to official test-set access restrictions
quantitative_results: HERMES overall: RFS 6.81, ADE@3s 0.82, ADE@5s 1.82, FDE@3s 1.73, FDE@5s 4.81
UniAD baseline: RFS 5.78, ADE@3s 6.50, ADE@5s 10.81, FDE@3s 12.14, FDE@5s 21.41
VAD baseline: RFS 4.45, ADE@3s 3.19, ADE@5s 5.81, FDE@3s 5.85, FDE@5s 12.30
LightEMMA baseline (zero-shot): RFS 6.11, ADE@3s 3.07, ADE@5s 5.41, FDE@3s 6.07, FDE@5s 11.32

Category-wise global RFS: HERMES 6.81 > LightEMMA 6.11 > UniAD 5.78 > VAD 4.45; HERMES per-category examples: single-lane 7.28, construction 7.26, pedestrian 7.11, cyclists 6.78, interaction 6.96, multi-lane 6.71, special vehicles 6.72, cut-ins 6.54, others 6.41, FOD 6.37

Training config: single RTX 4090, batch size 16, 3 epochs, Adam lr 1e-4 (warmup 1e-5, cosine to 5e-6), grad clip L2=5.0, MSE loss over 5s/20 future timesteps, 16 past frames, Risk Planning Cross-Attention alpha=0.3, images 224x224
baselines_compared: UniAD (query-based transformer, adapted to WOD-E2E interface)
VAD (vectorized scene representation, adapted to WOD-E2E interface)
LightEMMA (VLM-driven end-to-end, evaluated zero-shot without fine-tuning)
